The cell's repair machinery then either disables the gene (via non-homologous end joining) or inserts a new sequence (via homology-directed repair). Since 2013, CRISPR has been applied to human diseases including sickle cell disease, beta-thalassemia, and certain cancers. In December 2023, the FDA approved Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel), developed by Vertex Pharmaceuticals and CRISPR Therapeutics, as the first CRISPR-based therapy for sickle cell disease. The cell's repair machinery then either disables the gene (via non-homologous end joining) or inserts a new sequence (via homology-directed repair). Since 2013, CRISPR has been applied to human diseases including sickle cell disease, beta-thalassemia, and certain cancers.
